artifacts.content:
Hi Claire,

Finally sitting down with lukewarm tea while the kids argue over whose turn it is to feed the cat. I’ve been turning something over in my head and wanted to run it by you before I get too far.

I keep thinking about those evenings at Mom and Dad’s in Asheville after your miscarriage at Prisma Health Greenville Memorial in October 2023 and my own loss back in March 2021. As awful as that season was, there was something really grounding about sitting on their back porch with the little journaling prompts, just listening to the wind in the trees and not having to “be okay.”

I’ve started wondering if there’s a way to gently offer that kind of space to others. I’m picturing a small series of grief-support nature walks in Fall 2025 on the High Falls and Triple Falls loop at DuPont. Nothing clinical, just 2–3 hour, slow walks with built-in quiet reflection time at the High Falls overlook where I’ve gone to cry more than once. I’d adapt the same prompts we used at Mom and Dad’s, maybe add a few about seasons changing, water moving over rock, etc.

Part of me worries it’s too personal, or that I’m not “qualified” beyond my own experience. But another part feels like this is exactly why I started Blue Ridge Adventure Guides in the first place—to help people use these places the way we have.

How would you feel about me mentioning our story in a very general way when I talk about why I’m offering these? And would you ever want to come along for one, or is that just way too much?

Call when you get a minute that isn’t taken up by patients or laundry.

Love,
M

metadata.subject: Idea for gentle grief hikes at DuPont

artifacts.content:
Hi Dr. Patel,

I hope you’re well. I wanted to share something I’m working on and get your quick take, mostly as Evan’s mom but also knowing you see a lot of kids like him.

I’m developing a beginner nature photography walk at the Hooker Falls access area in DuPont that’s specifically geared toward neurodivergent kids—basically, kids like Evan with ADHD and generalized anxiety. Since his diagnosis at Mission Children’s Hospital in Asheville back in May 2022, we’ve seen how much better he does when things are predictable and broken into small chunks.

So I’ve mapped out a 2.5-hour route from the Hooker Falls parking lot to the overlook and back that’s very low-stimulation: short segments of walking, then a 10-minute “movement and reset” break where they can jump, spin, or just mess with their cameras. I’m planning to use a simple visual schedule similar to what Ms. Karen Lewis, the counselor at Hendersonville Elementary, suggested after Evan’s panic episode on the playground last October—pictures for “walk,” “rest,” “photo time,” etc.

Evan has been my unofficial test pilot, and it’s the first time I’ve seen him stay regulated for that long on a trail. He actually said, “I knew what was coming next, so my brain didn’t get all buzzy.” That felt huge.

Is there anything I should be mindful of from your perspective—length of time, wording when I talk about ADHD/anxiety with parents, any red flags? I’m trying hard not to overpromise but still make it welcoming.

No rush on a detailed response; even a quick “seems fine” when you have a gap between patients would help.

Best,
Melissa Donnelly
Blue Ridge Adventure Guides, LLC

metadata.subject: Nature photography walks for kids with ADHD/anxiety

artifacts.content:
Hi Mom and Dad,

Quick update while I wait for a group to finish taking photos at Triple Falls.

We’re still juggling the car situation, but it’s working better than I expected. Since the second vehicle got repossessed by Carolina Community Credit Union last June after we fell three months behind between the loan and the commercial auto insurance for Blue Ridge Adventure Guides, we’ve had to get creative. On weekday mornings when I have a Lake Imaging hike, Daniel loads everyone into the Subaru, drops my group at the Lake Imaging access area trailhead, then heads straight to Hendersonville High for his 8:00 a.m. chemistry class. I hike the group back out and either catch a ride with a local guide friend or wait for Daniel to swing back by after school.

It’s not glamorous, but it’s keeping trips on the calendar and money coming in. We’ve cut way back on eating out and random Target runs, and I’m putting every extra dollar toward a small emergency fund so we’re not that close to the edge again.

On the bright side, the kids seem fine with it. Evan thinks it’s “epic carpooling logistics,” and Grace just likes any excuse to see Daniel in his classroom if we happen to overlap.

Mom, how are you feeling energy-wise this week? If you’re up for it, I’d love for you to meet us at High Falls access on Saturday so you can hang with the kids at the picnic area for a bit before I take the clients on the longer loop.

Love you both,
Melissa

Program sketch – grief-support nature walks (personal draft)

Working title: “Quiet Miles: Walking with Grief at DuPont”

Audience: Adults who’ve experienced pregnancy loss, miscarriage, or other close bereavement. Keep it small—max 6 participants.

Timing: Fall 2025, Sunday afternoons when the light is softer and the crowds thin out a bit.

Route: High Falls access → High Falls Trail → Triple Falls trail section → back. Aim for 2–3 hours total, very gentle pace, lots of pauses.

Anchors:
- Start with a grounding moment at the High Falls overlook. Mention my own losses (March 2021) and Claire’s miscarriage at Prisma Health Greenville Memorial in Oct 2023 only if it feels appropriate and not self-centered.
- Use journaling prompts adapted from what we did at Mom and Dad’s house in Asheville: “What are you carrying today?”, “Where do you feel your grief in your body?”, “What would you say to your person if they were walking beside you?”
- Build in quiet stretches where no one has to talk.

Logistics:
- Bring extra notebooks/pens, tissues, thermos of tea.
- Emphasize I’m not a therapist; this is a guided walk with space for reflection.
- Check DuPont advisories beforehand; avoid days with high water near Triple Falls.

Personal reminders:
- If someone gets overwhelmed, it’s okay to pause and sit with them.
- Have a short list of local grief counselors in Asheville/Hendersonville handy.
- After each walk, debrief at home (journal, tea, maybe a short yoga flow) so I don’t carry everyone’s stories to bed.

Hi Jenna,

I’m excited about your company’s retreat day in DuPont this June. I’ve been sketching out options for your 20-person group coming from Asheville.

For logistics, I strongly recommend we meet at the Hooker Falls parking lot. It’s one of the few spots where bigger vehicles can pull in and load/unload without everyone feeling like they’re in a game of Tetris. When we’ve had larger vans or a charter bus, that lot has been the least stressful. It does get busy on weekends, but if we start early we can usually beat the worst of the crowding.

My plan is to split your folks into two smaller hiking groups (10 and 10, including guides). One group can start on the Hooker Falls Trail for an easy warm-up to the falls overlook, while the other heads toward Triple Falls, then we swap. Pace will be easy-moderate with lots of photo stops and short interpretive breaks about forest ecology and waterfall safety.

I also want to flag that the Hooker Falls area is popular with families, so I’ll build in extra time for bathroom breaks and general milling around. If you’d like, we can add a short mindfulness moment by the river—nothing woo-woo, just a quiet few minutes to listen to the water.

Send me your preferred date range and whether there are any mobility issues or strong fears of heights in the group. Once I have that, I’ll lock in a route and send a packing list you can forward to everyone.

Best,
Melissa
Blue Ridge Adventure Guides, LLC

metadata.subject: DuPont retreat logistics – Hooker Falls meetup

artifacts.content:
Post-workshop debrief – Waterfall photography group 05/10/2025

Location: High Falls / Triple Falls loop from High Falls access
Group size: 7 clients + me

What went well:
- Staggered start times at each viewpoint kept things from feeling crowded.
- The “phone-only” segment actually got people to slow down and think about composition.
- Safety briefing at the trailhead landed better when I framed it as “I want you to go home bragging, not limping.”

Challenges:
- As usual, once people got into “photographer mode,” they started drifting. A couple of folks edged off the main tread and onto the damp rocks near the water to try to get a lower angle shot. I had to call them back more than once. Need a stronger, more specific reminder that tripods and feet stay on the established path, no exceptions.
- Hard to balance giving people space to spread out along the banks for different angles with keeping an eye on everyone’s footing.

Changes for next time:
- Add a second safety reminder at the first big stop: “If you wouldn’t walk there without a camera, don’t walk there with one.”
- Consider bright tape on my trekking poles to mark a “do not cross” line for the group at tricky spots.
- Maybe add a brief segment about how erosion and trampling affect riverbanks so it doesn’t sound like I’m being arbitrary.

Personal note:
- I felt my anxiety spike when I saw someone step toward the slick rock. Take three deep breaths before correcting so I don’t sound snappy.

metadata.title: Debrief: Waterfall photo workshop – High/Triple Falls

artifacts.content:
Event: High Falls family-friendly hike + Mom pickup

Date: Saturday, 2024-06-22
Time: 9:00 a.m. – 1:30 p.m.
Location: High Falls access area parking lot, DuPont State Recreational Forest
Attendees: Melissa, clients (Smith family – 4), Evan, Grace, Mom (Pat)

Agenda / notes:
- 8:00–8:30 a.m.: Load gear at home (214 Cascade Overlook Dr). Snack bags for kids, extra water for Mom.
- 9:00 a.m.: Meet Smith family at High Falls access parking lot. Quick intros + safety talk.
- 9:20 a.m.: Start up High Falls Trail at very easy pace so Mom can join for the first stretch.
- Remember: since her mild stroke at Mission Hospital in Asheville in Feb 2024, Mom’s only supposed to walk about 20–30 minutes at a time. Build in a short rest at the picnic area near the access lot.
- 9:45–10:00 a.m.: Picnic area stop. Let Mom sit with Evan and Grace for snacks and photos. Make sure she has her folding chair and a shady spot.
- 10:00 a.m.: Daniel picks Mom + kids up at the parking lot to take them back to Hendersonville.
- 10:05 a.m.–1:00 p.m.: Continue longer High Falls/Triple Falls loop with clients only. Emphasize waterfall safety and Leave No Trace.
- 1:00–1:30 p.m.: Wrap-up at parking lot, answer questions, hand out business cards.

Reminder to self: check in with Mom Friday night about how she’s feeling; if she’s tired, adjust so she just meets us at the picnic tables and skips the initial walk.

metadata.title: High Falls family hike + Mom pickup
metadata.location: High Falls access area parking lot, DuPont

artifacts.content:
Brainstorm – “Off the Beaten Path” offerings (DuPont)

Goal: Create a couple of advanced adventure options for repeat clients who’ve already done High Falls/Hooker Falls basics and want something that feels more wild, without turning it into a circus.

Ideas:
- Market as “off the beaten path” half-day adventures for strong hikers (4–6 people max). Emphasize solitude, deeper dive into ecology, and more strenuous terrain.
- Consider using a few of the lesser-known social paths that climb above Triple Falls to reach quieter viewpoints. They’re not on the official map, but they do get occasional local use and feel much more remote. Need to think carefully about when/if this crosses a line with DuPont’s trail guidelines.
- Build in a strong ethics component: why we avoid widening these paths, no cutting new routes, strict single-file hiking, absolutely no scrambling down toward the top of the falls.

Other angles:
- Pair one of these days with a mindful photography focus for experienced shooters (no tripods near edges). Remind them of past issues with people wandering onto slick rock for “the shot.”
- Offer these only on weekdays to minimize overlap with big crowds at the main overlooks.

To do:
- Talk to two other local guides about how they’re handling “secret spots.”
- Revisit insurance policy from Blue Ridge Outdoor Liability & Casualty to confirm coverage for routes on unofficial but established use trails.
- Draft website copy that’s honest but not hyped.
